In [3]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid")
In [5]:
df = pd.read_csv("netflix_titles.csv")

df["date_added"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date_added"], errors="coerce")
df["title_length"] = df["title"].str.len()
df["date_added_year"] = df["date_added"].dt.year
df["duration_minutes"] = df["duration"].str.extract(r"(\d+)").astype(float)

df.head()

In [6]:
print("Shape:", df.shape)
print("\nColumns:")
print(df.columns.tolist())

print("\nMissing values:")
print(df.isnull().sum())

print("\nDuplicate rows:")
print(df.duplicated().sum())
Shape: (8807, 15) Columns: ['show_id', 'type', 'title', 'director', 'cast', 'country', 'date_added', 'release_year', 'rating', 'duration', 'listed_in', 'description', 'title_length', 'date_added_year', 'duration_minutes'] Missing values: show_id 0 type 0 title 0 director 2634 cast 825 country 831 date_added 98 release_year 0 rating 4 duration 3 listed_in 0 description 0 title_length 0 date_added_year 98 duration_minutes 3 dtype: int64 Duplicate rows: 0
In [7]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
plt.hist(df["release_year"], bins=20)
plt.title("Distribution of Netflix Titles by Release Year")
plt.xlabel("Release Year")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.show()
Out[7]:
In [8]:
type_counts = df["type"].value_counts()

plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
plt.bar(type_counts.index, type_counts.values)
plt.title("Number of Titles by Type")
plt.xlabel("Type")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.show()
Out[8]:
In [9]:
plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))
sns.countplot(data=df, x="rating", order=df["rating"].value_counts().index)
plt.title("Frequency Distribution of Netflix Ratings")
plt.xlabel("Rating")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()
Out[9]:
In [10]:
grouped_data = df.groupby("type")["release_year"].mean().sort_values()
print(grouped_data)

plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
plt.bar(grouped_data.index, grouped_data.values)
plt.title("Average Release Year by Type")
plt.xlabel("Type")
plt.ylabel("Average Release Year")
plt.show()
type Movie 2013.121514 TV Show 2016.605755 Name: release_year, dtype: float64
Out[10]:
In [11]:
titles_by_year_type = df.dropna(subset=["date_added_year"]).groupby(["date_added_year", "type"]).size().unstack(fill_value=0)

titles_by_year_type.plot(marker="o", figsize=(12,6))
plt.title("Number of Titles Added to Netflix by Year and Type")
plt.xlabel("Year Added to Netflix")
plt.ylabel("Number of Titles")
plt.show()
Out[11]:
In [12]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.scatterplot(data=df, x="release_year", y="title_length", alpha=0.6)
plt.title("Relationship Between Release Year and Title Length")
plt.xlabel("Release Year")
plt.ylabel("Title Length")
plt.show()

movies_df = df[df["type"] == "Movie"].dropna(subset=["duration_minutes"])

plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.scatterplot(data=movies_df, x="release_year", y="duration_minutes", alpha=0.6)
plt.title("Relationship Between Release Year and Movie Duration")
plt.xlabel("Release Year")
plt.ylabel("Duration (Minutes)")
plt.show()
Out[12]:
In [13]:
corr_df = df[["release_year", "title_length", "date_added_year", "duration_minutes"]].corr()

plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
sns.heatmap(corr_df, annot=True, cmap="Blues", fmt=".2f")
plt.title("Correlation Heatmap of Numerical Variables")
plt.show()

print("Summary and Conclusion")
print()
print("This analysis shows that the Netflix dataset contains more movies than TV shows.")
print("Most of the content was released in more recent years, and Netflix added many titles over time.")
print("The rating chart shows that some audience ratings appear much more often than others.")
print("The scatter plots and heatmap show that some numerical relationships are weak, while others show small patterns.")
print("Overall, the dataset gives a clear picture of Netflix content by type, year, rating, and duration.")
Out[13]:
Summary and Conclusion This analysis shows that the Netflix dataset contains more movies than TV shows. Most of the content was released in more recent years, and Netflix added many titles over time. The rating chart shows that some audience ratings appear much more often than others. The scatter plots and heatmap show that some numerical relationships are weak, while others show small patterns. Overall, the dataset gives a clear picture of Netflix content by type, year, rating, and duration.
